category: Unicode/encoded directory traversal to invoke cmd.exe (Code Red/Nimda-style RCE)
evidence: Multiple source IPs sent requests using double-URL-encoded (e.g. .%252e/.%252e), %35c, %5c, and overlong Unicode (e.g. %e0%80%af, %c0%af, %c1%9c) directory traversal sequences through /scripts/ to reach /winnt/system32/cmd.exe and execute 'dir' commands. These are classic IIS/Nimda/Code Red-style remote command execution exploits that attempt to bypass path canonicalization to reach the Windows shell; even though the target is a Linux (Fedora) Apache server, the payloads are unambiguous active exploitation attempts.
sample_entry: [Fri Jun 10 11:40:14 2005] [error] [client 213.205.73.192] Invalid method in request get /scripts/..%e0%80%af../..%e0%80%af../..%e0%80%af../winnt/system32/cmd.exe?/c+dir

category: IIS root.exe backdoor/RCE probe (Code Red II)
evidence: Repeated GET requests to /scripts/root.exe?/c+dir and to the canonical Code Red/Nimda traversal set (/scripts/root.exe, /MSADC, /c, /d, /_vti_bin, /_mem_bin, /msadc, plus encoded traversals like ..%5c.., ..%2f.., ..\xc1\x1c.., ..\xc0\xaf.., ..\xc1\x9c..). These are the well-known Code Red II / Sadmind worm fingerprint used to drop/invoke a cmd.exe-equivalent backdoor on IIS servers and enumerate drive letters. Although this server runs Apache on Fedora, the payloads are in-flight exploitation attempts, not benign scans.
sample_entry: [Sat Jun 11 12:00:08 2005] [error] [client 202.118.167.71] Invalid method in request get /scripts/root.exe?/c+dir

evidence: A single IP hammered /var/www/cgi-bin/awstats, /var/www/cgi-bin/awstats.pl, /var/www/cgi-bin/stats, /var/www/html/awstats/awstats.pl, /var/www/html/stats/awstats.pl, /var/www/html/awstats.pl and /var/www/html/cgi over many seconds with hundreds of requests. This is automated scanning for the AWStats CGI script (awstats.pl), which has a history of RCE/information disclosure vulnerabilities (e.g. CVE-2005-0116, CVE-2005-1527) and is a very common web-application target. The volume (115+ hits from a single host) and enumeration across multiple common install paths confirm a targeted vulnerability scan rather than a typo.
sample_entry: [Sat Jun 11 03:03:03 2005] [error] [client 202.133.98.6] script not found or unable to stat: /var/www/cgi-bin/awstats

evidence: The source IP issued 20 consecutive requests to /var/www/cgi-bin/openwebmail in a tight burst (~10 seconds). OpenWebMail has a history of critical remote vulnerabilities (e.g. shell metacharacter injection in openwebmail.pl that yields RCE). Repetitive probes specifically for this CGI path are characteristic of automated vulnerability scanning/exploit tooling looking for a misconfigured or unpatched OpenWebMail installation.
sample_entry: [Sat Jun 11 14:32:51 2005] [error] [client 212.238.198.203] script not found or unable to stat: /var/www/cgi-bin/openwebmail

evidence: Repeated 404s for /_vti_bin (and companion paths _mem_bin, MSADC, msadc, /c, /d) are the classic footprint of the Nimda/Code Red IIS-worm and FrontPage-extension vulnerability scans. In particular 81.214.165.213 made 23 hits specifically against /_vti_bin in a single burst, and 210.91.137.35 combined /_vti_bin probes with an overlong URI (possible buffer-overflow attempt against FrontPage). These are not normal user traffic; they are automated scans for vulnerable IIS/FrontPage endpoints.

Three independent IPs each generated a tight burst of 15-20 identical 404s for /var/www/html/sumthin within a single second. '/sumthin' is not a resource that legitimate clients would request; this pattern is typical of automated bot/script probing (often used as a canary, a noise test, or by old worm variants) and indicates reconnaissance against the web root.

category: Sustained directory probing / web-root enumeration (repeated 403s from single IPs)
evidence: Each of these IPs generated 9-22 'Directory index forbidden by rule' errors against /var/www/html/ in a short window (seconds to a minute). A single 'directory index forbidden' can be a misclick, but many rapid-fire requests from one host to the web root, from geographically dispersed IPs, indicates automated directory enumeration / scanner activity against the server.
sample_entry: [Mon Jun 13 18:38:25 2005] [error] [client 195.23.79.241] Directory index forbidden by rule: /var/www/html/

evidence: Three source IPs triggered 'request failed: URI too long (longer than 8190)' errors. In two of the three cases this immediately followed probes for /_vti_bin, and in one case (_vti_bin + overlong URI) it is consistent with historical FrontPage / IIS buffer-overflow exploit attempts that send very long URLs to overflow fixed-size buffers. Even when the server correctly rejects the request, deliberately exceeding the maximum URI length is hostile traffic that warrants flagging.
sample_entry: [Sun Jun 12 05:05:35 2005] [error] [client 210.91.137.35] request failed: URI too long (longer than 8190)
